speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape five of the interview with Anna Reich. It's the 7th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready. It's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I consider what I did think then and judge Mrs. Schindler as a very humane sort of person and was [? just-- ?] everything had with the basket. And I always did think she does what she can to help us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was next to this Schindler establishment in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- was a warehouse with-- which was a storehouse of wool, knitting wool. And sometime, somehow it was a bit of wool smuggled. And I remember knitting a cardigan for one the SS women for trying to get some-- maybe something, more food or whatever, [BACKGROUND NOISE] in the nighttime, very tiny light. I did knit this cardigan. I remember. It was a very nice cardigan in a navy color. [LAUGHS] And I also did knit some socks, because sometime the Czech people, which was close to this establishment of Schindler, was bringing-- gettings-- I've done-- some exchange was going on, and I gave them the knitted socks. And they brought sometime maybe the rice or maybe some sort of food. This I-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you get in return for the cardigan?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I can't remember. I-- I only remember that she did think it was very nice. That's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your contact with the guards at the camp, was that different from at Plaszow? Were they more human?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would say yes. They was not friendly by all means, but by far more easy. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you at the camp for? At Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Schindler?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we came sometime during November or early December. What I-- you see, there was no calendar. There was no interest in time or date, you know? And even though I did have a hope of survival, I still did not follow the time-- the dates. And we have been liberated in May '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- [SIGHS] what I-- what I understood then, [? and ?] it was-- we-- we're not supposed to be left behind. And what I understood then, there was grave-- there was people taking-- for the job or whatever. But what I understood, there was some grave in the woods for us to be shot at. And what I heard that Schindler did-- have-- he always did drink with those SS people and all the people which was in charge and you know. And I understand he'd have a great party that night and they drank and drank and drank. And I remember hearing the artillery louder and louder. And what I understand, in the morning, it was-- or whenever they did want to take us to shoot us, it was too late. The front did progress very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next thing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, which is a day before, we did see a black-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh god, what you call it? Flag-- a black flag. I was wondering what happened. And somehow the rumors that gets to us that Hitler died, yeah? And-- What did you feel then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know it's true or not, but we did think it must be something, you know, to see the black flag. And then we did hear [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] getting louder and louder, all these artillery noises. And in the morning, I remember I had been surprised that it wasn't any appelle. You see, I wasn't so close to all this, what happened. I surprised there wasn't appelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next thing, I remember we came on this yard, where usually we had been counted. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see any guards. It was-- where are they? Where are they? It was-- what's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard that the Russian had come. And in about few hours or-- I can't recall exactly-- maybe two hours or three hours, this first Russian man on a horse did enter our camp. I remember him very vividly. He'd have a clock on on this white horse and the Russian-- you know, the Persian hat-- cossack hat. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, the propaganda, the Russian propaganda, was always then so strong. So his first word was that-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] we Russian liberated you, and you are free. And we-- we did have to pinch each other to believe it. And then soon after did come all the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] big things, Stalin and, you know, all those heroes of the Russian-- the communists. [LAUGHS] And, of course, they did try to straightaway [? persuade, ?] persuade us how good the regime that is. And I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they come in the troika, [LAUGHS] those Russian soldiers. And they invited me to step in for a ride. And I was terrified.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they say, don't worry. Don't worry. And [LAUGHS] they took me for a ride. I was at the back, and they was in the front. And they did have a big bottle 4711, all their cologne. And the whole bottle I did pour it on me, you know? I was terrified then, because, you know, the Russian didn't have such a good name for morality then.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] But I remember they probably, in their own way, they did want to make me happy that way. And-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a warehouse where also materials was, and every prisoner got three meter of this material, I remember. And and that was the beginning of being fluid, [LAUGHS] being financial.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a lot of German people leaving in-- and they said to us, you take-- you can take the liberty to go to their homes and take what you want and all this. But-- I don't know-- I-- All I was dreaming is to put my head in a bag of sugar and have enough [LAUGHS] bread to eat, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember a Russian soldier giving me a gun. And there was young boys in the army, children. As they progressed, they took children into the army to survive it, because the German exterminated villages. And, you know, whoever was survived, they took the kids to get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he gave me the gun, and he said I can shoot for my parents. [SOBBING] And I can shoot whoever I wanted. But I never-- I-- I always did have the ideas, not that I want to say something good about myself, that two wrong don't make one right, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as the German, there was also going back, they was in a terrible condition. And with the children, they did have to evacuate and go to Germany. And they was also looking terrible and all this, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Poland after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Prague because there was a Red Cross, a very big Red Cross in Prague. And people was looking for each other. And I couldn't find anybody. And from Prague, [LAUGHS] I remember one incident. It was when I was in Prague. There was a funeral. I was with my friend from concentration camp. And there was a funeral. And must have-- some stateman must have died. Because there was music and flowers and, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And me and my friend, we start to laugh so hysterically because [LAUGHS] of the one person die and having such a pompous funeral [LAUGHS] and that we have been hysterically laughing, you know? We couldn't stop. We fell over with laughters because so comical that it appeared to us. Yes. And from Prague, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Prague, I went to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did get from Red Cross such a-- a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] card that shine-- no-- anyway, that we can-- that we come from the concentration camp and that we can travel without paying. And I remember we-- I went as far as Krakow. And in Krakow, I came, and then I went on the train in Krakow. And I showed the conductress this card that I am allowed to-- and she said, oh, the Jews are coming back. That was her reaction. And my reaction was the big decision then-- the next day I'm leaving Poland. I also-- when I find out about my auntie, who was married to this doctor that she lives in Stalowa Wola, which was [SIGHS] in sight, and I did take a train to go there to see her. And I remember from the train, they was taking Jewish people and shooting them-- the Polish-- the Poles-- some Anders-- I don't know. They-- I heard the name of Anders' Army. But I don't know they was them or not. But I know there was great fear between them that when the Jews come, they would climb their properties or whatever. So they-- some of them survived all that and have been shot on the way to their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is true. And I come and visit my auntie. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I decide-- I told her-- she was with her husband. He was a doctor then in this hospital. And she did have a baby not long before. And she's now here in Australia. Of course, she's a grown-up person after all this time-- very much grown up. And I said, you know, we're leaving Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Gliwice-- Gliwice. It was part of the-- from Krakow to Gliwice. In Gliwice, we stay very little while. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We did some trading to get some money together and all this. And then we went to Szczecin, because we did want to go to Berlin, because we heard that a lot of people meeting there and have big family-- family reunions and Red Cross, and there was such rumors. So our aim was to come to go to Berlin. And in Szczecin, we did approach Russian soldiers, which was in a car, like-- what should I say-- military car. And we asked them where they was going. And they say they're going to Berlin. And we asked them if they would take us, and they said, oh, all right. We say we-- we said to them that we're coming from the concentration camp. And we-- and he said, all right. And they took us. And my husband did have a wonderful voice. He was a great tenor. And he all the time through the border-- smuggled us through the border-- they was singing all the time. And they brought us to Berlin, which was so bombed out and was terrible to come there. And they gave us as a departure a sack of lard, which was absolute fortune then, because the German people didn't have anything to eat, and it was terrible. And this lard-- we had been dropped in front of a synagogue. But it was late. And this synagogue, it was nobody there. And I remember a postman was coming. And we were standing just there, not knowing where to go. And he said-- he ask us, where do we [? go? ?] And say, we don't know where to go. We just arrive. And we-- and he said, come on. I give you my place. I accommodate you. And he was very nice, because this place was bombed out, and he did have only a very small place for himself and the one little room, where it was like [LAUGHS] very, very narrow, that only a bed-- when we did want to go to bed, we did have to step from the end to-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, German. Yes. And he was very nice, I must say, because his daughter was bomb-- when it was bombing, she was burned. And I remember she was in a dreadful condition. And he took her from this room to the kitchen and gave us the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must say-- I must say things like that. Of course, the German cried rivers to us, that who was it and how is that possible and [SIGHS] all this, you know, of course. That they didn't know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- most-- they said they didn't mostly. Mostly they said they didn't. But they must have known something. I mean, the propaganda was such a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in-- Berlin--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Berlin, from this lard, cutting small bits, we-- this postman spread the word [LAUGHS] that we had the lard. And they pile us with gold coins to get a piece of lard, you know? And that's how our fortune start, [LAUGHS] from this sack of lard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later we got accommodation. And I must say, Berlin was very, very quickly built. They was working day and night to remove the ruins and to start to build-- unbelievable-- unbelievably organized, you know? I tell you. [SIGHING] I met the-- anyway, the-- and-- So you were there for--?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband started a business. It was a import of cocoa and margarine from Holland. And it was very prosperous. We got accommodation. And then first in Russian sector, then we move to the American-- to the English sector and then to the American sector. And we have been prosperous. And we did need this prosperity to recover somehow on [SIGHS] [? mind ?] and body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stay in Berlin almost five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you briefly tell me about coming to Australia and what you ended up doing in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, then it was through the IRO that we did--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the IRO?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was organization, a refugee organization, which-- my husband did always want to go to Australia. We could have chosen America or Australia. I did want to go to the United States, but my husband as a young boy, he always did dream about Australia. You know, adventurous sort of thing. And we decided to come to Australia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't easy, because I did have friends, which was educated. She was a teacher. And he was also a educated man. And today was put down for Australia, because then the immigration did look for the muscle into Australia, not the educated people. They didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave us the hint, don't tell them you're educated, because you wouldn't have a chance. So we just come on the contract here. It was my husband from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I must say, from the good life that it was for us in Berlin, we come to the Bonegilla, which was a migrant camp, which was terribly primitive. And there was polio then, epidemic of polio, and my daughter got sick, and my husband got dysentery. And she was taken to hospital, and I said-- and we didn't have any document yet, but I said, documents or not, I don't care. I with another friend-- my husband was sick. I took a train and come to Sydney without the document to look for some accommodation, which, of course, was then Christmas 1950, almost impossible to get accommodation. [SIGHS] We came to a real estate agent. I believe his descendants must be, because he was in Bondi. [BACKGROUND NOISE] His name was Rosen. I believe he's still there, probably his grandson. And he told us-- he told me, [INAUDIBLE] look, I can't give you any accommodation. But if you want-- because we-- we did have some money. We didn't come here then very poor. We did have some money, even in Switzerland by then, because we accumulate quite a lot of money in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, oh, I can only offer you-- what they call the-- with two next to each other? A-- a unit? Apartment or a unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or it's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: We might have to wrap it up a bit, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway-- [LAUGHS] anyway, he finally-- we find some [INAUDIBLE] accommodation in what is called Wright. And then my husband took ill. He was operated on, slipped disc. And it was a very hard beginning. He was in a plaster and was not well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the money was going away, and our doctor, who attend to my husband, said-- I brought beautiful wardrobe with me from Berlin. And I said, you know, Mrs. Reich, you dress so well. Why don't you open a frock shop where I come from, and that's Eastwood?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know what he was talking about. Anyway, I did-- I did meet the lady who did have a frock shop. And I-- she took me there for what was supposed to be training. And it was very hard to get any shop, but whatever I did have in jewelry, I did have to put in as a guarantee and got finally this little, tiny shop in Eastwood, which did--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many stores did you end up getting in?

speaker: interviewee
text: --which did prosper and was great success. And then at one stage we did have four, and I did employ at one stage about 21 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I-- then here I am today.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, my daughter, when we come to Australia, she was three--

speaker: interviewer
text: We might just have to leave it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera is-- camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at this part.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say when you're ready. You introduce us all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my right is my daughter Sylvia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left is my son Gregory. Further down is my granddaughter Jackie and then my second granddaughter Katherine and my son-in-law Pete Eisman, Dr. Pete Eisman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you want to tell us about the people in the photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my husband, who passed away two and a half years ago. It would be almost three years. It would be in June three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and this is Daniel, my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is on excursion.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on an excursion and would love to have been here and was very disappointed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I [INAUDIBLE] we couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's great that you could all be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we wanted to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hold it still, Sylvia. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We wanted to be here and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's a lovely boy. Pity he couldn't do-- make it, but that's just how it is. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we're all here after--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's really important.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the end of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is me, I would say of age of-- when I was three-year-old in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three-year-old-- must have been 1923?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my little sister Pola. That's my mother. That's me, age of probably six. This my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva. She did not survive, nor did my auntie-- nor did my sister, my only sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin Lucinka, who was-- who really suffered and died as a martyr. And that's her little brother, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here she was about five. And he was maybe 18 months or 20 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's my Auntie Eda, who is still alive. She's 92. She'll be 93, and she's in Montefiore Home in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she must have been about 25, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, in the botanical garden probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my picture. I think that's my first picture after the war in [INAUDIBLE] [? Gliwice ?] Gliwice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Gliwice?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-L-I-W-I-- It's Gliwice now, because it was before German. Now it belongs to Poland, so it finish with C-E. Gliwice. We had to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is on the boat, Casta Bianco, on the way to Australia. And that is 1950-- no-- December 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my husband is not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's a picture of me and two models when we did a fashion parade for a charity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1968, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: With clothes from your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an authentic coat from concentration camp, Auschwitz, which my aunt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] managed to hold it all this time. It did meant so much to her. And I took it from her. Oh, God-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she found?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt has a great story to tell. She survived the most unique way. They was walking from Auschwitz, running away from the front, and without food and being very exhausted, there was a lot of dead people on the way. And those who it was-- apparently it was one car, a military car or whatever, and [? in ?] those very exhausted one, they put on that car. And they was-- when they die, they unload the car and new, very exhausted one, the new candidates, came on that car. And she was on this car at this stage, and she did have a thought that next time when they unload the car, she would pretend she's dead and fall out with the dead people. And so she did. She was semi-conscious and unconscious when suddenly she did-- lying there between the dead, she heard people, voices. But she-- it was unfamiliar language to her. And she think, this is not German, somehow in her-- not-- half-consciousness. And there was American, which did find her between the dead people. And she moved, and that's how she was survived. They took her and sent her to hospital, where she was for quite long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape five, which was the last tape of the interview with Anna Reich. It's the 7th of March, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
